title: Dependency Injection
objectives: Explain DI vs the Service Locator pattern

sections.markdown: Dependency Injection means an object receives its collaborators from outside instead of creating them. This decouples classes, enables testing with fakes, and is the practical form of the Dependency Inversion Principle (SOLID).

Constructor DI is the strongest form: a class's dependencies are visible in its signature, making it obvious what to fake in tests. Service locators (`GetIt.I<...>()`) are convenient but hide dependencies and can cause runtime surprises.
Registering everything as a global singleton. Long-lived state leaks across screens/users and breaks tests. Scope thoughtfully: app / feature / screen.

flashcards.back: Classes receive their collaborators from outside instead of creating them.
Constructor DI is explicit and testable; Service Locator is convenient but hides dependencies.
To avoid global state that leaks between users/features and breaks tests.

quiz.q: The preferred form of DI is…
quiz.explain: Constructor DI makes dependencies explicit and easy to fake.

quiz.q: A Service Locator's biggest downside is…
quiz.explain: You can't tell a class's dependencies from its signature.
Global lifetime keeps state alive too long.

challenges.text: Rewrite a class that creates its own Api to receive it via the constructor.
Wire the app using GetIt (Service Locator) and note the trade-offs.
Wire the app using Riverpod providers with proper scopes; swap in fakes for tests.
Build a per-feature scope where each screen gets its own ViewModel instance while a Repository is shared.

interviewQuestions: Why is over-scoping to Singleton bad?
How do Riverpod/Hilt/GetIt compare as DI tools?
